29: hymeis de tina me legete einai — but who do you say that I am? — the crucial Christological question placed at the Gospel's structural midpoint (ch.8 is the pivot between Galilee-ministry and Jerusalem-passion in Mark). su ei ho Christos — you are the Christ — Peter's confession without the fuller Matthew expansion (Son of the living God). Immediately after the confession, the Messianic Secret is commanded (v.30) and the passion prediction follows (v.31), connecting Messiahship directly to suffering and rejection. In Mark, you cannot confess the Messiah without immediately being told about the cross.
34: ei tis thelei opiso mou akolouthein, aparnessastho heauton kai arato ton stauron autou kai akoloutheitō moi — if anyone wishes to come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross and follow me — the three imperatives are sequential: (1) aparnessastho heauton (deny himself = disown the self's claims on its own life); (2) arato ton stauron autou (take up his cross = adopt the posture of the condemned man carrying his instrument of execution); (3) akoloutheitō moi (follow me = continue in discipleship to the one walking to his death). Cross-bearing in Mark's context is not metaphor but literal: the disciples are called to the same path of suffering their master walks.
1: Kai palin pollou ochlou ontos kai mē echontōn ti phagōsin — and again with a great crowd and they having nothing to eat — palin (again) links the second feeding explicitly to the first (6:35–44). The setting is implied Gentile territory east of Galilee (7:31 placed Jesus in the Decapolis). The pastoral crisis — large crowd, no food — is structurally identical to the first feeding, which will make the disciples' confusion in v.4 more pointed.
2: Splanchnizomai epi ton ochlon — I have compassion for the crowd — splanchnizomai (to be moved in the innards — the strongest Greek emotion-word; the word for gut-level compassion) is the same verb as in 6:34 (Jesus's compassion before the first feeding). The repetition is not accidental: both feedings are driven by the same inward motivation. The duration: three days (treis hēmeras) without food. The fast is already extended beyond the point of polite hunger into physical necessity.
3: kai tines autōn apo makrothen hēkasin — and some of them have come from far away — makrothen (from a distance, from afar) appears in Ephesians 2:13,17 and Acts 2:39 for Gentiles who were "far off" but are drawn near in Christ. The word does not prove the crowd is Gentile but is consistent with the Decapolis location. The physical risk is genuine: they will be exhausted (eklythēsontai en tē hodō = will be undone on the road) without food on the journey home.
4: kai apekrithēsan autō hoi mathētai autou hoti pothen touton dynēsetai tis hōde chortasai artōn — and his disciples answered him: from where will anyone be able to satisfy these with bread in this isolated place — the disciples' question mirrors the first feeding question (6:37): the logistical impossibility is stated again despite the prior miracle. Mark presents the repetition without explanation; only in 8:17–21 will Jesus name the disciples' failure to draw the inference as spiritual hardness. erēmia (isolated place, wilderness) is the word for the desert that echoes the Exodus feeding-tradition.
5: hepta artous — seven loaves — versus five in the first feeding (6:38). The difference in numbers (and the different basket-counts that follow) supports reading the two feedings as distinct events rather than doublets: five and twelve are associated with Israel (five books of Moses; twelve tribes/apostles); seven and seven are associated with completeness/Gentile fullness (the Gentile nations). Mark preserves both accounts with the numerical differences intact.
6: kai paraggellei tō ochlō anapesein epi tēs gēs — and he instructed the crowd to recline on the ground — the seating arrangement echoes the first feeding (6:39–40: the green grass, the groups). kai labōn tous hepta artous eucharistēsas eklasen kai edidou tois mathētais autou hina paratithōsin — and having taken the seven loaves, having given thanks, he broke them and gave to the disciples to set before the crowd — eucharisteō (to give thanks — the verb that becomes the technical term for the Eucharist in Paul's institution-account: 1 Cor 11:24; Luke 22:17,19). Mark uses both eulogēō (6:41, in the first feeding) and eucharisteō (8:6) across the two accounts; the Eucharistic vocabulary is in both, not only the second.
7: kai eichon ichthydia oliga, kai eulogēsas auta eipen kai tauta paratithenai — and they had a few small fish, and having blessed them he said to set these before them also — the fish are handled separately with their own blessing (eulogēsas). The separation of bread and fish in the account maintains the two-element structure of both feeding accounts, consistent with the tradition being independently transmitted and not a scribal copy of the first account.
8: kai ephagon kai echortasthēsan kai ēran perissemata klasmatōn hepta spyridas — and they ate and were satisfied, and they picked up seven baskets of broken pieces — spyris (large flexible basket — the type used to lower Paul over the Damascus wall: Acts 9:25) is different from kophinos (6:43; the standard Jewish basket, mentioned by Juvenal as the prop of Jewish street-vendors in Rome). The basket-type change between the two accounts, combined with the location in Gentile territory, supports reading the second feeding as directed toward a Gentile crowd — though Mark does not make this explicit.
9: Ēsan de hōs tetrakischilioi — and they were about four thousand — the smaller number (4,000 vs. 5,000) is one of several numerical differences between the two feeding accounts that Jesus will recall precisely in vv.19–20 as evidence the disciples should have understood the significance. kai apelysen autous — and he dismissed them — the crowd is sent away; the mission moves on.
10: kai euthys embas eis to ploion meta tōn mathētōn autou ēlthen eis ta merē Dalmanutha — and immediately getting into the boat with his disciples he came to the region of Dalmanutha — Dalmanutha is otherwise unattested in ancient sources; Matthew's parallel (15:39) reads Magadan/Magdala. The location is likely on the western shore of Galilee, Jewish territory, which explains the immediate Pharisaic encounter in v.11.
11: kai exēlthon hoi Pharisaioi kai ērxanto syzētein autō, zētountes par' autou sēmeion apo ouranou peirazōntes auton — and the Pharisees came out and began to argue with him, seeking from him a sign from heaven, testing him — syzēteō (to debate jointly, to argue against) indicates adversarial engagement, not honest inquiry. sēmeion apo ouranou (a sign from heaven) contrasts with the earthly healings already performed: they demand a cosmic confirmatory sign of a different order. peirazō (to test, to tempt) is the same word as Jesus's wilderness testing by Satan (1:13) — the Pharisees' demand is framed as Satanic-scale opposition to Jesus's mission.
12: kai anastenaxas tō pneumati autou legei, Ti hē genea hautē zētei sēmeion? — and sighing deeply in his spirit he says: why does this generation seek a sign? — anastenaxas (sighing deeply — intensified form of stenazō from 7:34, with ana- prefix adding depth) is located tō pneumati autou (in his spirit — the deep inner seat of the person), not merely a physical sigh. The sigh registers the profound spiritual failure the demand represents. amēn legō hymin, ei dothēsetai tē genea tautē sēmeion — the amēn oath-formula (ei + unfulfilled condition = "if a sign will be given... [it will not]") is an absolute refusal with the strongest possible assertive form. genea (generation, contemporaries) is used pejoratively throughout Mark's central section (8:38; 9:19).
13: kai apheis autous palin embas apēlthen eis to peran — and leaving them again getting in he departed to the other side — the abrupt departure without further engagement characterizes Mark's ending of controversy scenes with the Pharisees: they receive no debate, no sign, no engagement — only the flat refusal of v.12 and Jesus's immediate departure. The crossing to the other side removes him from their vicinity without explanation or farewell.
14: kai epelathonto labein artous, kai ei mē hena arton ouk eichon meth' heautōn en tō ploiō — and they forgot to take bread, except they had one loaf with them in the boat — the disciples' forgetfulness is the occasion for Jesus's teaching in vv.15–21. The hena arton (one loaf) in the boat is noted with precision; the disciples' anxiety about it in v.16 is ironic given the two feedings immediately preceding. Some interpreters read the "one loaf" as a Christological symbol — the One Bread who is with them in the boat — but Mark does not make this explicit.
15: horate, blepete apo tēs zymēs tōn Pharisaiōn kai tēs zymēs Hērōdou — watch out, beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and the leaven of Herod — zymē (leaven, yeast) as metaphor for pervasive invisible influence: a small amount leavens the whole batch (1 Cor 5:6; Gal 5:9). The pairing of Pharisees and Herod combines religious and political opposition: Pharisees are the religious establishment seeking a sign (v.11); Herod is the political authority who executed John (6:14–29) and who was troubled by reports about Jesus (6:16). The leaven-metaphor names their shared characteristic: a corrupting influence that spreads undetected.
16: kai dielogizonto pros allēlous legontes hoti artous ouk echousin — and they reasoned with one another saying: it is because we have no bread — the disciples hear "leaven" (zymē) and immediately think bread (artous) — a misinterpretation on the level of Nicodemus hearing "born again" and thinking of physical birth (John 3:4). Their reasoning (dielogizonto — from dialogizomai = to reason through, same root as dialogismos in 2:6) is entirely literal; they have reduced a spiritual warning to a logistical concern about food supply.
17: kai gnous legei autois, Ti dialogizesthe hoti artous ouk echete? — and knowing he says to them: why do you reason that you have no bread? — gnous (having perceived, having known — the immediate inward perception also used in 2:8 and 5:30) is Jesus's characteristic spiritual awareness of what is unspoken. oupō noeite oud' syniete? pepōrōmenēn echete tēn kardian hymōn? — do you not yet perceive or understand? is your heart hardened? — pepōrōmenēn (hardened — perfect passive participle of pōroō = to harden, callous; pōros is a callus or calcification) is the Isaiah-language for spiritual insensibility (Isa 6:10; cf. Mark 3:5 where Jesus grieves the Pharisees' hardened hearts). The disciples are diagnosed with the same condition as their opponents.
18: ophtalmous echontes ou blepete kai ōta echontes ouk akouete? kai ou mnēmoneuete? — having eyes you do not see, and having ears you do not hear? and do you not remember? — the seeing-without-seeing and hearing-without-hearing are the prophetic diagnosis of covenant-hardened Israel (Jer 5:21; Ezek 12:2) now applied to the Twelve. The disciples have been given the mystery of the kingdom (4:11) but their perception remains blocked. The third question (kai ou mnēmoneuete = and do you not remember?) anticipates the recall of the two feedings in vv.19–20.
19: hote tous pente artous eklasen eis tous pentakischilious, posous kophinous klasmatōn plēreis ērate? — when I broke the five loaves for the five thousand, how many baskets of fragments did you pick up? — the numerical recall is precise: five loaves, five thousand, twelve kophinus. The disciples answer correctly (dōdeka). The two feedings are not interchangeable events; the distinct numbers (five/twelve; seven/seven) are the evidence that should have generated theological inference about who was performing them.
20: Hote tous hepta eis tous tetrakischilious, posōn spyridōn plērōmata klasmatōn ērate? — when the seven for the four thousand, how many baskets of fragments did you pick up? — the basket-word changes: spyridas (vs. kophinous in v.19) matching the actual accounts (6:43 kophinus; 8:8 spyridas). The disciples' correct answers (hepta = seven) prove they remember the factual details; what they lack is the capacity to interpret them. The One who fed thousands twice is in the boat with them — bread-anxiety is incoherent.
21: kai elegen autois, Oupō suniete? — and he said to them: do you not yet understand? — the pericope ends on an open question, not a declaration. suniemi (to understand, to put it together — the capacity to synthesize evidence into comprehension; used for the disciples' failure throughout: 4:13; 6:52; 7:18) is the word for what the two feedings, correctly interpreted, should have produced. The question hangs: the answer is expected of the reader as much as the disciples.
22: Kai erchontai eis Bēthsaida, kai pherosin autō tuphlon kai parakalosin auton hina autou hapsētai — and they came to Bethsaida, and they brought him a blind man and begged him to touch him — Bethsaida (on the northeastern shore of Galilee, Philip's tetrarchy) is outside Jewish Galilee. The healing-by-touch request (hapsētai) echoes the hemorrhaging woman's approach (5:27). The blind man is brought by others — the same pattern as the Syrophoenician daughter (7:25) and the deaf-mute (7:32).
23: kai epilabomenos tēs cheiros tou typhlou exēnenken auton exō tēs kōmēs — and taking the blind man by the hand he led him outside the village — the withdrawal to privacy before healing (cf. 7:33 taking the deaf-mute apart from the crowd). Jesus leads the blind man by the hand — physical guidance as well as healing. ptysas eis ta ommata autou (having spat into his eyes) — spittle applied directly to the eyes, a physical healing medium consistent with 7:33. kai epitheis tas cheiras autō epērōta auton, Ei ti blepeis? — and having laid hands on him he asked him: do you see anything?
24: kai anablepas elegen, Blepō tous anthrōpous hoti hōs dendra horō peripatountas — and looking up he said: I see the people, for as trees I see them walking — partial sight: the people are visible but unfocused, tree-sized and indistinct. This is the only Markan miracle requiring two applications; the first laying-on-hands produces incomplete healing. The structural positioning is significant: the two-stage healing follows the disciples' two-stage understanding (they know who Jesus is — v.29 — but cannot yet grasp the cross), anticipating that full sight will require a second encounter with Christ's person and work.
25: eita palin epethēken tas cheiras epi tous ophtalmous autou, kai dieblepen kai apekatestē kai eneblepsen tēlaugōs apanta — then again he laid hands on his eyes, and he looked intently and was restored and saw everything clearly — apekatestē (was restored completely — aorist passive of apokathistēmi = to reconstitute, restore fully; the same root as apokatastasis, the complete restoration of all things) and tēlaugōs (clearly, at a visible distance — the adverb of far-shining clarity) describe the completed state: full restoration of clear long-distance vision. The progression from no sight to partial sight to complete sight maps the spiritual journey that will continue through the next section of the Gospel.
26: kai apesteilen auton eis oikon autou legōn, Mēde eis tēn kōmēn eiselthēs — and he sent him to his home saying: do not even enter the village — the Messianic Secret extended: not merely silence-commanded but direction-given (eis oikon = to your home). The healed man is sent back to domestic space, not into public space where news would spread. The restriction matches 5:19 (the Gerasene told to go to his house) and 7:29–30 (the Syrophoenician mother goes home to find her daughter healed).
27: kai en tē hodō epērōta tous mathētas autou legōn autois, Tina me legousin hoi anthrōpoi einai? — and on the road he was asking his disciples saying to them: who do the people say that I am? — the Caesarea Philippi question is placed at Mark's structural midpoint: chapters 1–8 are the Galilean ministry of power; chapters 9–16 are the journey to the cross. The question on the road (en tē hodō) anticipates the journey-to-Jerusalem that will define the rest of the narrative. The popular identifications (v.28) all cast Jesus as a prophetic figure — John the Baptist, Elijah, one of the prophets — none identifies him as the Messiah.
28: hoi de eipon autō legontes hoti Iōannēn ton Baptistēn, kai alloi Elian, alloi de hoti heis tōn prophētōn — they said: John the Baptist; others: Elijah; and others: one of the prophets — the three identifications map the prophetic horizon of expectation. The John the Baptist identification echoes Herod's fearful supposition (6:14,16). The Elijah identification connects to Malachi 4:5 (Elijah will return before the great Day of the Lord) — a widespread Second Temple expectation. The generic prophetic category casts Jesus in the tradition of the great OT spokespersons. None of these captures his identity; all project a known category onto him.
30: kai epetimēsen autois hina mēdeni legōsin peri autou — and he charged them that they should tell no one about him — the Messianic Secret command follows the only correct identification. The command is now absolute and immediate: the correct confession (su ei ho Christos) triggers not proclamation but silence. The silence command makes the passion-prediction that immediately follows (v.31) the authorized context for understanding what kind of Messiah he is; without the cross, the confession is incomplete.
31: kai ērxato didaskein autous hoti dei ton huion tou anthrōpou polla pathein kai apodokimasthēnai hypo tōn presbyterōn kai tōn archiereōn kai tōn grammateōn kai apoktanthēnai kai meta treis hēmeras anastēnai — and he began to teach them that it is necessary for the Son of Man to suffer many things and to be rejected by the elders and the chief priests and the scribes and to be killed and after three days to rise — dei (it is necessary, it is divinely obligatory — the word for divine necessity throughout Luke-Acts, John, and the NT passion-predictions) is the key term: the suffering is not accidental, permitted, or merely foreseen — it is divinely ordained. The three-part Sanhedrin (presbyteroi = elders; archiereis = chief priests; grammateis = scribes) is the complete Jerusalem authority that will execute the condemnation. Meta treis hēmeras anastēnai (to rise after three days) is stated as clearly as the rejection and death.
32: kai parrēsia ton logon elalei — and plainly/openly he was speaking the word — parrēsia (frank speech, bold openness — the opposite of veiled or cryptic communication; the speech of one who holds back nothing). The passion-prediction is stated with maximum clarity to the disciples, contrasting with the parable-speech of chapter 4 which concealed from outsiders. kai proslabomenos ho Petros auton ērxato epitiman autō — and Peter taking him aside began to rebuke him — epitimaō (to rebuke, to charge sternly) is the word Jesus uses for rebuking demons (1:25; 3:12) and storms (4:39); Peter uses it against Jesus. The inversion is the narrative shock of the scene.
33: ho de epistrapheis kai idōn tous mathētas autou epetimēsen Petrō kai legei, Hypage opisō mou, Satana, hoti ou phroneis ta tou theou alla ta tōn anthrōpōn — and turning and seeing his disciples he rebuked Peter and says: get behind me, Satan, because you do not think the things of God but the things of men — phroneis (you think/are minded — from phroneō = to set one's mind on, to be oriented toward; opposite of tou theou phronein that Paul calls the Spirit's mindset in Rom 8:5–6). ta tou theou vs. ta tōn anthrōpōn — the divine vs. human perspective on the Messiah is absolute: the divine plan includes the cross; the human perspective is repelled by it. The identification of Peter's protest with "Satan" (Satana) links this scene with 1:13 and 8:11 — resistance to the cross is Satanic at the structural level, regardless of the speaker's intent.
35: hos gar ean thelē tēn psychēn autou sōsai apolesei autēn, hos d' an apolese tēn psychēn autou heneken emou kai tou euangeliou sōsei autēn — for whoever wants to save his soul/life will lose it; but whoever loses his soul/life for my sake and the gospel will save it — psychē (soul/life — the single Greek word covering biological life and the eternal person; the polysemy is essential to the saying). The saying operates simultaneously on two levels: physical life (saved by denying Christ = ultimately lost; surrendered for Christ = ultimately saved) and eternal life (the same logic at a deeper level). The double-level meaning is irreducible; neither level cancels the other.
36: ti gar ōphelei anthrōpon kerdēsai ton kosmon holon kai zēmiōthēnai tēn psychēn autou? — what does it profit a man to gain the whole world and to suffer loss in respect of his soul? — kerdainō (to gain profit — commercial vocabulary for profitable transactions) and zēmioō (to suffer loss, to be penalized — the corresponding loss-term; used in the papyri for fines) frame the calculation as a commercial exchange. The whole world on one side; the psychē on the other. The calculation is impossible: no sum of material acquisition compensates for the forfeiture of the person.
37: ti gar dōsei anthrōpos antallagma tēs psychēs autou? — for what will a man give as an exchange for his soul? — antallagma (exchange, a compensating value — from anti-allassō = to exchange in place of) appears in Job 28:15 LXX where no gold or silver can be given as antallagma for wisdom. The rhetorical form is a second unanswerable question: there is no antallagma, no possible counterweight for the psychē. The soul's value exceeds any convertible equivalent. The commercial metaphor is completed: no bargain recovers what is lost when the soul is forfeited.
38: hos gar ean epaischynthē me kai tous emous logous en tē genea tautē tē moichalidi kai hamartōlō, kai ho huios tou anthrōpou epaischynthēsetai auton — for whoever is ashamed of me and my words in this adulterous and sinful generation, the Son of Man will be ashamed of him — epaischynō (to be ashamed of, to feel the disgrace of association with — the verb of public dissociation in an honor-shame culture). In the first-century Mediterranean world, to be "ashamed of" someone is to publicly distance oneself from them to preserve one's own honor-standing. The Christological demand is stated as an inverse: if you deny the Messiah before humans to preserve social honor, the Son of Man will deny you at the judgment. hotan elthē en tē doxē tou patros autou meta tōn angelōn tōn hagiōn — when he comes in the glory of his Father with the holy angels — the coming-in-glory is the Daniel 7:13 enthronement event (cf. 13:26; 14:62); the holy angels attend the cosmic judgment scene (cf. Matt 25:31). The Son of Man who now walks to the cross will come in the Father's glory: the full reversal of the current hiddenness.
